title: SnoposNet: Sleep Posture Recognition from Snoring Signals
authors: Dongsheng Li, Wenlong Liu
publish: 2026 IEEE 8th International Conference on Communications, Information System and Computer Engineering (CISCE)
abstract: Sleep posture is closely related to the severity of obstructive sleep apnea-hypopnea syndrome, making accurate posture recognition important for sleep assessment and intervention. Snoring signals present a noncontact solution for posture recognition due to their physiological correlation and straightforward acquisition. However, existing audio models lack specialized designs for snore signal analysis. This paper proposes SnoposNet, a novel framework built upon an audio Transformer. The model employs a snore time-frequency decoupling module to model the subtle, posture-induced acoustic variations from temporal and spectral domains, alongside a gender-aware feature aggregation module that adaptively modulates the acoustic feature representation using gender information. The proposed SnoposNet achieves an accuracy of 93.80% and F1-score of 94.28% on the SSBPR dataset, demonstrating its effectiveness and potential for accurate, noncontact sleep posture recognition.

title: Implement Augmented Reality (AR) Interfaces in Disaster-Resilient Edge-Cloud Computing: An Innovative Interactive System for Emergency Operations First Responders
authors: B. Nancharaiah, Ali Hussein Ali Abu Zaid, B. Shravan Kumar, Talluri Upender, Rajeev Sobti, G Karuna
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICDSAAI)
abstract: The ability to analyse the situation swiftly, communicate consistently, and make informed decisions under time limits is a vital skill in disaster response. In addition to dealing with known dangers, first responders face obstacles such as damaged infrastructure, decreased visibility, and a lack of communication. Latency, connectivity dependence, and data overload are potential issues with traditional emergency management solutions that depend on human data processing and centralised cloud services. Emergency operations first responders can make real-time, interactive decisions using AR interfaces and edge-cloud computing in this unique disaster-resilient architecture. Even when cloud connectivity is intermittent, edge computing processes time-critical data locally to deliver low-latency responses. The cloud provides large-scale data analytics, coordination, and historical data integration. AR interfaces show responders contextual, spatially aligned information, including hazard locations, victim status, navigation cues, and team coordination data in their field of vision, reducing cognitive load and improving efficiency. The suggested architecture prioritises durability, scalability, and adaptability to maintain operations amid infrastructure failures. Response time, situational awareness, and decision accuracy are better than those of conventional emergency response systems in experiments. A study found that AR and edge-cloud computing improve disaster response safety, coordination, and efficacy, making emergency management systems more resilient.

title: Stochastic Analysis of Cramér-Rao Lower Bound for Positioning in mmWave-THz HetNets
authors: Jiajun He, Yiyong Sun, Feng Yin, Wenxin Xiong, Hing Cheung So, Hien Quoc Ngo, Hyundong Shin, Michail Matthaiou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: Terahertz (THz) frequency band has been widely studied and is recognized as a promising candidate for centimeter-level localization. However, the limited coverage of THz networks may result in localization failures, while a heterogeneous deployment of millimeter-wave (mmWave) and THz radio units (RUs) offers a viable solution to mitigate this issue. This paper presents a theoretical framework for evaluating the performance limits of localization systems in mmWave and THz heterogeneous networks. In this architecture, the mmWave RUs serve as macro base stations (BSs), while the THz RUs function as micro BSs distributed around each mmWave RU. By leveraging the standard tools of stochastic geometry to model the spatial distributions of the RUs and ambient obstacles, the localizability of a target is computed to evaluate the probability of achieving sufficient signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio for localization in both line-of-sight (LoS) and non-line-of-sight (NLoS) conditions. Furthermore, the Cramér-Rao lower bounds in both LoS and NLoS scenarios are analytically derived to characterize the overall positioning performance. Numerical results demonstrate that the hybrid deployment strategy significantly improves both the network coverage and localization accuracy compared to mmWave-only and THz-only networks.

title: Capacity-Optimized Design of Reconfigurable Antenna Patterns for MU-MIMO Systems
authors: Cuicui Zhang, Ming Zhang, Xiaoming Chen, Xiaobo Liu, Shitao Zhu, Anxue Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Reconfigurable antennas have emerged as a promising solution in wireless communication systems to adapt to dynamic environments and enhance system performance. Pattern reconfigurability plays a crucial role in improving antenna adaptability to spatially diverse multi-cluster channels, thus maximizing channel capacity. Existing works primarily address either the mode selection problem from a pre-designed finite pattern set which adjusts only beamwidth and scanning direction and thus can not offer optimal channel adaptation, or the mode design problem of computing continuously adjustable patterns under the assumation of an infinite pattern number but without considering the limit on the pattern number in real-world antennas. To address this gap, this paper proposes a practical design methodology that jointly accounts for the finite number of reconfigurable patterns and their precise optimization over diverse channel conditions. Specifically, channels are grouped based on user location distribution, and a joint optimization approach combining alternating optimization, auxiliary variables, and bisection is employed to design the optimal pattern set under the constraint of limited reconfigurable states. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the optimized patterns and show that the achieved capacity exhibits an approximately logarithmic relationship with the number of reconfigurable patterns, thereby providing a practical validation of existing theoretical analyses. This work bridges the gap between theoretical mode design and practical implementation by offering a concrete and implementable solution for designing a finite set of reconfigurable antenna patterns tailored to realistic system constraints.

title: Next-Generation Solid-State Transformers for Powering Artificial Intelligence Data Centers: A Survey of Emerging Topologies
authors: Jonas Huber, Johann W. Kolar
publish: IEEE Industrial Electronics Magazine
abstract: The rapid growth of AI datacenters and the transition toward megawatt scale power consumption of single server racks demand a fundamental redesign of datacenter power distribution architectures. Conventional low voltage ac (LVac) systems face increasing limitations in efficiency, scalability, and material usage. Solid state transformers (SSTs) offer a pathway to compact medium voltage ac to low voltage dc (MVac LVdc) conversion, enabling MVac distribution directly to the server racks. However, today’s industrial fully modular SST demonstra-tors show limited power density of roughly 0.2 MW/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>, which is insufficient for supporting the targeted high compute densities. This paper surveys emerging single-stage and quasi single-stage SST topologies with the potential for breaking through the power density barrier and achieving 1 MW/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> at 99% efficiency. Alternatives based on low-frequency transformers for near term adoption of LVdc distribution are also discussed. The paper highlights the need for a holistic comparative benchmarking of the different distribution architectures together with protection methods and SST concepts, especially including future MVdc distribution and MVdc-LVdc SSTs close to the server racks.

title: A Vision Transformer–GAN Framework for Robust Gait Recognition under Multi-Gait Conditions
authors: Shuvo Pramanik, Anonno Singha Ray, Sarafat Hussain Abhi
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Signal Processing, Information, Communication and Systems (SPICSCON)
abstract: Gait recognition has emerged as a promising biometric for unobtrusive and long-range person identification, yet its effectiveness is significantly challenged under multi-gait conditions involving occlusions, view variations, and noisy silhouettes. In this work, we propose a hybrid framework that integrates a Vision Transformer (ViT)-based classifier with a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)-driven silhouette reconstruction module to achieve robust recognition across diverse gait scenarios. A structured preprocessing pipeline employing YOLOv8 and Mask R-CNN ensures consistent silhouette generation, while GANbased refinement mitigates occlusion artifacts and enhances input quality. Experiments on the SMVDU-Single-Gait and SMVDU-Multi-Gait datasets demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance compared to CNN- and RNN-based baselines, with the ViT model attaining a test accuracy of 82.2% and a test loss of 0.466. These results highlight the effectiveness of transformer-based representations, combined with adversarial refinement, in advancing robust gait recognition under realworld, multi-gait conditions.

title: AI-Powered Smart and Secure E-Assessment Framework for Academic Institutions
authors: C Jackulin, A Marybenita, A Karthikeyan, S Jebasudha, K Lokesh, M LokeshKumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICDSAAI)
abstract: The issue of exam control in educational facilities is still challenging, especially in online or distant environments where student authentication is often insufficient, surveillance is not regular, and exam honesty is challenging to ensure. These problems may result in unfair evaluations and jeopardise academic standards. To simplify online testing, we present an intelligent and secure e-assessment system in this paper that leverages contemporary web technologies and AI-based monitoring. With webcam and microphone verification, students can safely take exams that the faculty generate and upload. The system will be able to monitor student behaviour at the time of testing and detect potential malpractice through a variety of tools such as facial recognition, real-time video analysis, and anomaly detection. The platform tracks submissions, tracks progress, and provides administrators with valuable information in real-time. Pilot tests and experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our system in maintaining exam security, ensuring authenticity, and enhancing administrative efficiency. By combining AI-driven monitoring with convenient reporting and exam management, the system encourages fair evaluation and online assessment while enhancing academic integrity. Keywords-Academic Integrity, Online Exam, Facial Recognition, Secure E-Assessment System, AI-Powered Monitoring, and Real-Time Proctoring.

title: Design and Implementation of an ERP-Based Smart Car Rental Management System for Digital Tourism Platforms
authors: Phanintorn Suaprae, Pisit Chansuek, Thanakrit Janchidfah, Jittapoo Poolwan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Cybernetics and Innovations (ICCI)
abstract: The rapid growth of digital tourism services requires efficient platforms to manage transportation services such as vehicle rental. However, traditional car rental operations often rely on fragmented processes that reduce operational efficiency and data transparency. This study proposes the design and implementation of an ERP-based smart car rental management system integrated with a mobile application platform. The proposed system integrates key enterprise modules including customer management, vehicle inventory management, booking management, and payment processing within a unified ERP architecture. The system adopts a multi-layer architecture consisting of a user interface layer, application service layer, ERP core modules, and centralized data management. System usability and service performance were evaluated using data from 120 users through a five-point Likert scale questionnaire. The reliability of the evaluation instrument was verified using Cronbach's alpha. The results indicate that users reported a high level of satisfaction with the system (Mean <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=4.34, S D=0.69$</tex>), particularly in terms of usability and service efficiency. The findings demonstrate that integrating ERP architecture into car rental platforms improves operational coordination, data integration, and service transparency. The proposed system provides a scalable digital solution for transportation management within the tourism ecosystem.

title: AgentBRTA: An LLM and RAG-Based Conversational Agent for Bangladesh Road Transport Authority
authors: Md Naimur Rahman Khan Sifat
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Signal Processing, Information, Communication and Systems (SPICSCON)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have consistently shown unparalleled reading and writing capabilities of language, but their real-world application in special-purpose regulation situations must entail fusion of domain-specific knowledge to be relevant and precise. AgentBRTA, a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) oriented conversational agent, was designed to provide answers to Bangladesh Road Transport Authority (BRTA) policies and regulations. AgentBRTA draws on BRTA’s authoritative law documents, using semantic vector retrieval and reranking to pinpoint salient context and an instruction-specific LLM (GPT-4o-mini) to produce accurate, precise answers. BERTScore-based automated evaluation has AgentBRTA consistently scores 0.83, outperforming the benchmark GPT-4o (0.78) and GPT-4o-mini (0.69) baselines. Human evaluation testing also validated the system’s capability to produce coherent, informative, and factually accurate answers. AgentBRTA offers a real-world, scalable solution to facilitate increased citizen access to BRTA services and administrative ease

title: AMMHAS: An Adaptive Multimodal Framework for Mental Health Assessment with LLM-Based Interpretability
authors: M. Sumithra Bhargavi, Btpr Nikhil, M. Chandralekha, M Rahul Raj
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICDSAAI)
abstract: Evaluations of mental health have become increasingly dependent on self-reported methods that are prone to subjective biases. Recent advancements in artificial intelligence and affective computing have created novel opportunities for objective and data-driven evaluations via behavioral indicators This study proposes a novel Adaptive Multimodal Mental Health Assessment System that assesses expressions across various modalities, specifically textual, acoustic, and visual. Modality-specific feature extractors and deep learning models are employed, followed by multimodal fusion using a multi-layer perceptron to generate normalized emotion score distributions rather than single-label predictions. These scores are subsequently summarized into structured psychometric reports designed for interpretability. To enhance explainability, a lightweight large language model is integrated as a post-processing component to translate structured emotion metrics into human-readable, non-diagnostic explanations. An experimental evaluation on the benchmark multimodal emotion recognition dataset shows that multimodal fusion generates more stable and consistent emotion representation compared to unimodal approaches, highlighting the potential of AMMHAS as an explainable application-oriented mental health assessment framework.

title: From Classification to Localization: A Weakly Supervised Deep Framework for Interpretable Chest X-ray Analysis
authors: Shahad Bin Alamgir, Hafizur Rahman, Abhishek Sushil, Nilkantha Sushil Pranta, Pritom Sen Gupta, Zarin Rafah Chowdhury, Jannatul Mawa, Nurul Momin Resat, Arafat Uddin
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Signal Processing, Information, Communication and Systems (SPICSCON)
abstract: Chest X-rays (CXRs) remain an essential diagnostic modality for detecting thoracic diseases, yet manual interpretation poses challenges due to variability, complexity, and reliance on expert knowledge. This research introduces a novel deep learning framework that advances weakly supervised lesion localization and disease classification in CXRs through the integration of spatial attention mechanisms, anatomical priors, and probabilistic class activation mapping (PCAM) pooling. Our architecture incorporates a DenseNet-based feature extractor, a spatial attention module (SAM), and a pixel-wise segmentation model that isolates lung and heart regions to suppress irrelevant background features. The localized attention map is further refined via logical masking and fed into a PCAM pooling layer to generate disease-specific predictions with enhanced spatial interpretability. Trained and evaluated on the CheXpert dataset, our model achieves competitive Area Under the Curve (AUC) scores across multiple pathologies, closely approximating or exceeding established baselines. The results underscore the importance of anatomical constraint integration and probabilistic attention in improving diagnostic transparency and robustness. This work contributes toward developing clinically viable AI tools that align with radiological practices and offer scalable deployment in real-world healthcare settings.

title: Scalable Causal Tracing and Reproducible Episodes for Multi-Agent Systems
authors: Viswapriyan Ragupathy, B. Vijaya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICDSAAI)
abstract: Multi-Agent systems with Large Language Models enable complicated AI operations with distributed reasoning and coordination. The complex interactions between Multi-Agent systems challenge established observability paradigms. Agent-level coordination patterns, memory operations, and causal relationships required for debugging and optimising Multi-Agent systems are not supported by existing frameworks. MA-Trace is a complete observability framework that instruments causal linkages between agents, tools, and environments and ensures repeatable execution through deterministic replay. In MA-Trace, coordination graphs capture task delegation and negotiation structures, memory operation traces, and log episodic and semantic accesses with eviction reasons; environment contracts check state transitions with seed-controlled determinism. We establish innovative service level objectives as coordination efficiency, redundant action rate, and oscillation rate to evaluate multi-agent system health beyond existing metrics. Standard span models and exporters in OpenTelemetry provide compatibility with production observability infrastructure. ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks show 55% mean-time-to-insight reduction with sub-1% performance overhead. Compared to baseline systems, this approach reduces coordination overhead by $26 \%$, duplicated actions by $50 \%$, and oscillation rates by $48 \%$. Internal model reasoning and external coordination traces match $70 \%$ in causal path alignment validation. MA-Trace enables transparent debugging and systematic optimisation of complicated agent processes in multi-agent systems with production-ready observability.

title: XAI Based Early Menopause Detection using Super Learner and Discriminant Analysis
authors: Mercy Paul Selvan, S D Kalaivani, Kalyaniswari Parida, S. Sahana, Y Sai Lekhana, S. Prince Mary
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICDSAAI)
abstract: The early detection of menopause is essential for ensuring women’s good health status. This paper presents an Explainable Artificial Intelligence-based machine learning approach for predicting the early onset of menopause using various types of data, including physiological, biochemical, psychological, lifestyle, and text information. The machine learning techniques we will apply include Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA), Random Forest (RF), and Super Learner (SL), because they account for complex relationships among features. The main biological and psychological characteristics taken into account in our research include weight fluctuations, anxiety disorders, irritability, insomnia, irregular menstrual periods, and hot flashes. Additionally, our study will cover biochemical markers including estrogen, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), and body mass index (BMI). Moreover, we will investigate the effects of smoking, drinking alcohol, and physical exercise. To select appropriate features, we will adopt the LASSO approach. To achieve model interpretability, the SHAP framework will be employed. SHAP stands for Shapley Additive Explanations, a method for interpreting machine learning model outputs based on feature contributions. We will evaluate the performance of our models in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Area Under the Curve (AUC). The experimental results reveal the superiority of the Super Learner ensembles over single machine learning algorithms, thus illustrating the applicability of the ensemble learning approach for predicting early menopause onset. The experimental results show that the Explainable Artificial Intelligence approach is vital for making machine learning models more understandable and trustworthy for medical applications.

title: Multi-Objective Carbon-Aware Genetic Scheduling for Green University Data Centers
authors: Tanagrit Chansaeng, Pita Jarupunphol
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Cybernetics and Innovations (ICCI)
abstract: University data centers host heterogeneous AI and virtual-machine workloads, creating significant energy and carbon burdens. Existing schedulers optimize a single objective most commonly energy and ignore the temporal variability of grid carbon intensity (CI) and the diversity of service-level agreements (SLAs) across academic workload classes. This paper presents CA-MOGA, a Carbon-Aware Multi-Objective Genetic Algorithm scheduler that simultaneously minimizes (i) total energy consumption, (ii) operational carbon footprint, and (iii) SLA penalty, subject to server capacity and workload deadline constraints. CA-MOGA extends NSGA-II with a carbon-aware time-shifting operator that reschedules deferrable jobs to low-CI intervals. Evaluated on 192,720 real hourly server telemetry records from 22 physical servers spanning five academic workload types, with scheduling decisions driven by a calibrated synthetic carbon-intensity (CI) profile for the Thailand national grid (20 paired runs <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\times 12$</tex> stratified weeks), CA-MOGA achieves 16.6% energy and 21.3% carbon reduction versus a round-robin baseline (Wilcoxon p < 0.001). Against the energy-only GA, CA-MOGA delivers 2.0% further carbon reduction and 1.60 pp lower SLA penalty (p<0.001), demonstrating that the multi-objective carbon signal unlocks a portion of the trade-off space inaccessible to single-objective optimizers. Hypervolume analysis (HV=0.763 vs. 0.743 for Energy-GA) and ablation studies confirm the necessity of each CA-MOGA component.

title: A Calibration-Free Multi-Expert System for Automated Diopter Measurement: Overcoming Noise-Induced Binocular Inconsistency
authors: Thimaporn Phetkaew, Wattanapong Kurdthongmee, Wei-Jou Duh
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Estimating ocular alignment in unconstrained settings faces challenges due to Noise-Induced Binocular Inconsistency (NIBI) which results from random events like blinking, head motion and tracking noise leading to temporary inter-ocular inconsistencies. Existing solutions typically follow either a framebased or a classification-based pipeline which often fail to yield reliable results especially when required to produce continuous measurements for clinical interpretation. In this work, we introduce a calibration-free deep multi-expert architecture that is capable of estimating the required prism diopters in a matter of seconds, by analyzing a short monocular video clip in real-time. Unlike prior methods, we tackle ocular alignment as a temporal problem, which results in improved robustness. Our approach consists of a Geometric Expert (Living Ruler) which outputs iris-normalized and scale invariant features. These are followed by a Trajectory Expert that encodes both eye movement temporal dynamics and deep image features, which are then passed through a Stability Supervisor. The Supervisor — a fully convolutional Transformer encoder — outputs frame-wise reliability scores which are subsequently used to suppress any noise-induced inconsistencies. Due to the lack of publicly available datasets with corresponding prism diopter annotations, we simulate geometry-based reference deviation values for eyes with real human eye geometry from the Columbia Gaze Dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms other solutions in terms of both accuracy (MAE) of 1.3038Δ and R2 of 0.9963 for the range 0–50Δ and stability where a temporal fusion strategy leads to better results compared to frame-wise approaches especially when affected by transient noise. These findings indicate that continuous, calibration-free estimation of ocular deviation from standard video input is feasible, supporting potential applications in tele-screening and longitudinal monitoring.

title: Variable Selection-Based Recursive Method for Adaptive Quality-Related Fault Detection_supp1-3689128.pdf
authors: Yan Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Fault detection (process monitoring) for dynamic and nonlinear industrial processes, which can be viewed as complex systems, has become increasingly important in recent years. Under complex and dynamic operating conditions, traditional multivariate statistical process monitoring (MSPM) methods struggle to effectively capture the evolving behaviors of quality indicators at the system level. In this article, a novel variable selection-based adaptive MSPM method is proposed for quality-related process monitoring. First, an improved part mutual information (PMI) method is proposed for variable selection, categorizing process variables into quality-related and quality-unrelated groups, addressing the shortcomings of the original PMI being able to measure the correlation between only two variables. Second, a novel recursive kernel principal component regression (RKPCR) incorporating the hierarchical model order-reduction strategy is proposed to effectively track quality indicators. This method screens high-quality data for model updates based on distance-based classification and linear approximation, which mitigates model degradation caused by faulty data. In addition, a recursive kernel principal component analysis (RKPCA) method similar to RKPCR is proposed to monitor quality-unrelated faults. Finally, a numerical example and two industrial processes are used to demonstrate the performance of the proposed method.

title: Micro-Amplitude Wave Detection Based on Nonlinear Representation and Underdetermined Blind Reconstruction
authors: Lu Wang, Hao Li, Chunhui Zhao, P. Takis Mathiopoulos, Tomoaki Ohtsuki, Fumiyuki Adachi
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: In surface micro-vibration-based water–air cross-medium communication technology, existing research primarily focuses on idealized experimental settings involving single underwater sound sources and noise-free conditions. However, real-world ocean environments are often accompanied by multi-source interference, complex background noise, and nonlinear medium propagation, which pose significant challenges. To address these issues, this paper proposes an underdetermined blind source separation framework that combines a dual-feature-optimized generative adversarial network with subspace-based sparse feature extraction for surface micro-vibration signal detection. Specifically, a dual-feature-optimized U-Net GAN is designed to fuse temporal and time-frequency features, with blind source separation accuracy introduced as a feedback mechanism to guide adversarial training and improve the reconstruction of non-linear mixed signals. Subsequently, a subspace covariance-based mixing matrix estimation method is employed, coupled with a sparse-domain inverse transformation algorithm, to achieve accurate separation and reconstruction of source signals under underdetermined conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that the reconstructed source signals achieve 4.33% improvement in waveform similarity, 1.81 increase in source signal energy proportion, and 1.28 reduction in spectral distance compared to baseline algorithms.

title: Online Meteorological Forecasting Model Using Multi-feature Information Gain PSO and Evolving Fuzzy Systems
authors: Lei Hu, Xinghan Xu, Xingyi Miao, Jianwei Liu, Min Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Evolutionary Computation
abstract: Reliable meteorological forecasting is crucial for climate-resilient cities, renewable-energy integration, aviation and maritime operations, and public-health early warning. Modern sensor networks and reanalysis products generate multi-variate streams at fine temporal resolutions, where seasonal transitions, synoptic regimes, and extreme events can continuously reshape cross-variable dependencies and predictive relevance. Such nonstationarity and concept drift may inflate model complexity and degrade adaptability in evolving fuzzy systems (EFSs). To address this, we propose MIGPSO-EFS, an online forecasting framework that tightly couples a drift-aware binary feature optimizer with an interpretable EFS under a leakage-free sliding-window protocol. Specifically, an improved binary particle swarm optimizer periodically reselects informative lagged covariates via information-gain ranking, multi-group weighted sampling, importance-aware mutation, and adaptive threshold gating, while the downstream EFS evolves a compact rule base using density-based structure adaptation and updates consequents through fuzzy weighted recursive least squares with activation-ratio pruning for low-latency inference. Experiments on real-world datasets from five cities across minute-, hour-, and day-level resolutions, together with multi-horizon prediction and extreme-event level-grading evaluation, show consistent accuracy–efficiency advantages over representative evolving, deep-learning, and classical/hybrid baselines. Wilcoxon signed-rank tests further confirm that the improvements are statistically significant, demonstrating robust and practical online forecasting under drifting meteorological regimes.

title: Multi-pass Volume Reconstruction for Freehand Ultrasound: Demonstration in Simulation, Phantom, and In Vivo Kidney
authors: April Anlage, Qian Lin, Brian W. Anthony
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Objective: This work describes the development and validation of an algorithm to improve ultrasound volume reconstruction from noisy initial scans taken using a tracked freehand ultrasound device. Methods: The algorithm was first tested and refined in simulation using an approximate kidney shape model re-sliced to represent various real life scan conditions. Next, an Intel RealSense D435i camera was used to track the pose of a commercial ultrasound probe. This device was used to scan a phantom with a known volume and shape. Finally, repeated scans were taken in vivo to test the repeatability of volume measurements. Results: The simulation experiments were used to refine algorithm hyperparameters. The volume of the phantom was measured to within 2.5-17% (1-11.5% with automatic pass finding) of ground truth for four different scan styles, compared to traditional methods that underestimate the volume by 17.5%. In 11 human subjects, right kidney volume was measured within between 3.6-14.6% (7.7-23.3% with automatic pass finding) variation between scans. Conclusion: The proposed algorithm was demonstrated to produce repeatable volume measurement results in both phantom and in vivo scans. Significance: The proposed algorithm improves the clinical translatability and repeatability of freehand volume ultrasound systems.

title: OptiGait-LGBM: An Efficient Ensemble Learning Approach of Gait-based Person Re-identification in Non-Overlapping Regions
authors: Md. Sakib Hassan Chowdhury, Md. Hafiz Ahamed, Bishowjit Paul, Abu Bakar Siddique
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Signal Processing, Information, Communication and Systems (SPICSCON)
abstract: Gait recognition has in the recent past been receiving a lot of attention as an unobtrusive method of recognizing individuals at a distance. Video based gait systems can generally work with large benchmark data sets, but on real world and unconstrained data, their accuracy significantly drops. The environment conditions outdoors, changes in lighting, and dissimilar viewpoints of the camera are still the main issues, which are complicated by the necessity of computational efficiency. We fill this gap with OptiGait-LGBM, which is a skeletal-model method that is expected to be used in person re-identification under those conditions. It is based on appearance-invariant landmark features which transforms joint positions into compact numerical data to minimize memory consumption. We also provide alongside the model RUET-GAIT, a novel dataset of uncontrolled gait sequences recorded in complex outside environments. Comparative experiments of ensemble algorithms, such as Random Forest and CatBoost demonstrate that OptiGait-LGBM has training and testing accuracy of 81.1% and 77.4% respectively and 19 times faster training than CatBoost at low memory consumption. The model presented provides a resource-saving and convenient solution to gait recognition based on video mode in a real-world setup.

title: A Hybrid YOLOv7–Swin Transformer Architecture Optimized by Cuckoo and Pelican Algorithms for Diabetic Retinopathy Grading
authors: T. Kumaravel, T. Selvakumar, K. Venu, Kanmanipriya S, Madhuvanthi M, Malaravan R
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference for Convergence in Computing Technology (I3CTCON)
abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is a major source of preventable blindness, and early detection reduces risk of vision loss. Deep learning and its application in automated DR assessment appears promising; the most significant contributor to this is the choice and tuning of hyperparameters, which can be the most arduous and time-consuming portion of applying deep learning to a clinical decision. In this paper, we present a novel hybrid deep learning solution that utilizes a YOLOv7 based lesion detection network, combined with a Comprehensively Improved Trustworthiness-based Classifier, in a way that allows for both extensive examination and larger context analysis of the retina of an individual patient. In order to aid in the determination of optimal hyperparameters, we introduced two new metaheuristic strategies for the fine tuning of hyperparameters (such as learning rate, dropout rate, and batch size) based on a combination of new metaheuristic techniques (Cuckoo Search Optimization (CSO) and Pelican Optimization Algorithm (POA)). The results from our experiments indicate that the hybrid model with the baseline developed over combined APTOS 2019 + Kaggle DR datasets was achieved with 90.2% total error rate; and was increased to 95.1% and 94.7% when CSO and POA were applied, respectively, with corresponding increases in F1-scores. These results show the use of modern metaheuristic approaches to hyperparameter tuning can potentially enhance the predictive capabilities of advanced deep learning systems, thus providing a standard benchmark for DR automated grading.

title: AeroTrack - AI-Driven AQI Monitoring System
authors: Ansh Ralli, Divij Sharma, Godfrey Winster S
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference for Convergence in Computing Technology (I3CTCON)
abstract: Air pollution has become a critical global concern, significantly affecting environmental sustainability and human health. Most existing air quality monitoring systems face key limitations, including restricted sensor coverage, inconsistent data calibration, lack of real-time prediction capabilities, and limited user accessibility. Many rely solely on static monitoring stations, which provide delayed readings and fail to offer proactive health insights. Furthermore, current AI-based models, while accurate, often lack transparency and require high computational power, making them unsuitable for real-time deployment on consumer level devices. This paper presents AeroTrack, an intelligent, data driven system for real time air quality monitoring and forecasting. The system integrates environmental sensing, predictive analytics, and explainable artificial intelligence to provide accurate and interpretable insights into air quality variations. Realtime pollutant and meteorological data, including PM2.5, PM10, CO, and NO2, are collected via the World Air Quality Index (WAQI) API and processed through a Flask based backend. A Random Forest regression model is employed to predict future Air Quality Index (AQI) levels, achieving high predictive performance with low latency across multiple urban locations. To enhance model transparency, SHAP and LIME frameworks are incorporated to interpret the relative impact of key features on AQI outcomes. The React-based frontend delivers an interactive map driven dashboard for visualizing live and forecasted data, while an integrated chatbot module facilitates conversational access for end users. Comparative evaluation with baseline statistical models demonstrates that AeroTrack consistently outperforms traditional approaches in both accuracy and responsiveness. The proposed framework highlights the potential of combining machine learning and explainable AI to develop scalable, interpretable, and user centric systems for smart city environmental monitoring and sustainable urban planning.

title: Reducing Motion Artifacts in MRI: From Conventional Techniques to Deep Learning-Based Solutions
authors: Nishat Afroz Faizee, Nikita Shivhare Mitra
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference for Convergence in Computing Technology (I3CTCON)
abstract: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) has become a cornerstone of modern medical diagnostics, providing exceptional soft tissue contrast and detailed anatomical visualization without ionizing radiation. However, its clinical effectiveness is significantly compromised by motion artifacts— image distortions arising from patient movement during the extended acquisition periods inherent to MRI protocols. These artifacts manifest as blurring, ghosting, and signal intensity variations that can obscure critical diagnostic features, potentially leading to misdiagnosis, inconclusive results, or the need for costly repeat examinations. Conventional motion correction approaches, including respiratory gating, cardiac triggering, and navigator echo techniques, offer partial solutions but often require specialized hardware, extended scan times, or impose limitations on imaging protocols. The emergence of deep learning has introduced transformative possibilities for addressing motion artifacts through intelligent, data-driven methodologies that learn complex patterns directly from imaging data. This study investigates the application of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for automated brain tumor detection in MRI images, demonstrating how deep learning architectures can maintain diagnostic accuracy despite challenges inherent in medical imaging workflows. The methodology employs a carefully curated dataset of 400 brain MRI images divided into tumor and normal classes, implementing comprehensive preprocessing strategies including pixel intensity normalization and spatial standardization to 224×224 resolution. Advanced data augmentation techniques— rotation, zoom, and horizontal flipping—artificially expand the training dataset and enhance model generalization. A hierarchical CNN architecture featuring three convolutional blocks with progressively increasing filter depths (32, 64, 128) extracts multi-scale features, followed by fully connected layers with dropout regularization for classification. The model achieves 84% validation accuracy with 91% tumor recall, demonstrating robust performance on binary classification tasks. This research highlights the potential of deep learning solutions to complement or enhance traditional motion correction techniques, offering automated, scalable approaches that require no hardware modifications and can be integrated into existing clinical workflows, ultimately advancing diagnostic reliability in motion-compromised MRI examinations.

title: Material Strength Prediction Model for Weathering Steel Pipe Rod Based on Machine Learning Algorithm
authors: Hailong Liu, Dejian Yan, Kaicheng Shi, Weidong Pan, Ao Sun
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Recent Trends in Computing and Smart Mobility (RCSM)
abstract: Based on the challenge of time-consuming and inefficient traditional experimental methods for predicting weathering steel pipe rod strength, this study proposes a machine learning-based predictive model. To address this, we first systematically collected and preprocessed engineering data, then performed advanced feature engineering including correlation analysis, standardization, and crucially, constructing interactive features (e.g., carbon content × temperature) to capture complex nonlinear relationships. Subsequently, we compared algorithms (Linear Regression, SVM, RF) and implemented a dual ensemble learning strategy, combining Boosting and Stacking methods, to integrate model advantages and enhance robustness. Experimental results demonstrate the optimized Random Forest model combined with ensemble learning achieved superior accuracy (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>=0.95) and stability (RMSE=4.22 MPa) in predicting tensile strength, providing an efficient and reliable solution for rapid material strength assessment in engineering practice; this approach offers significant potential to replace conventional testing and efficiently guide production decisions.

title: A Few-Shot Transfer-Learning Approach for Industrial Defect Detection Using YOLOv12
authors: Bhagyashree A Mugalkhod, Sneha S Baragi, Sujith Roy V, Vikas Badiger, Uday Kulkarni
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference for Convergence in Computing Technology (I3CTCON)
abstract: Automated visual inspection is essential in modern manufacturing for ensuring product quality; however, existing deep learning models typically require large volumes of labeled training data, which is costly and time-consuming to obtain for rare or newly introduced defects. This paper proposes a transfer-learning YOLOv12 architecture optimized for industrial defect detection that learns from only 10 defective images per MVTec AD product category. The approach integrates a lightweight YOLOv12 backbone with a staged transfer-learning pipeline that uses data augmentation and class-balanced oversampling to improve detection accuracy under limited samples. Experiments on a held-out 20 percent subset of the MVTec AD dataset demonstrate that the proposed model achieves a mean average precision (mAP@50) of 0.636 under this few-shot dataset constructed by selecting 10 images per defect type, followed by category-wise defect localization, while maintaining real-time performance suitable for resource-constrained deployment. The framework reduces data annotation overhead and allows factories to adapt more quickly to new product lines and emerging defects, supporting practical industrial automation and quality control systems.

title: Towards Transparent Human Activity Recognition: An Evaluation of Machine and Deep Learning Frameworks Enhanced with Explainable AI
authors: Vinit Kumar, Yadunath Pathak, Manish Pandey
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Recent Trends in Computing and Smart Mobility (RCSM)
abstract: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) using wearable sensors is essential for healthcare monitoring, rehabilitation, and context-aware systems. This research proposes an interpretable and robust HAR framework that integrates traditional machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), and Explainable AI (XAI) techniques. The study uses the benchmark UCI HAR dataset containing tri-axial accelerometer and gyroscope signals from 30 participants. The methodology includes standardized preprocessing, feature extraction, and two modeling tracks. The ML track evaluates five classifiers - Logistic Regression, SVM, Random Forest, XGBoost, and k-Nearest Neighbors - using the official 561-feature representation. The DL track employs 1D-CNN and CNN-BiLSTM architectures trained on raw IMU sequences with early stopping. Models are assessed using the official test split and Leave-One-Subject-Out (LOSO) cross-validation across multiple metrics, including accuracy, macro-F1, precision, recall, specificity, and AUC-ROC. Explainability is provided through SHAP for ML models and Integrated Gradients and occlusion sensitivity for DL models. Robustness and ablation studies further evaluate generalization under signal noise, sampling variations, and feature/channel removal. Results show that hybrid DL models achieve superior accuracy while maintaining interpretability through XAI visualizations. Overall, the proposed framework delivers a balanced and reliable approach for dependable human activity recognition.

title: Axial-Field Switched-Reluctance Machine for In-Wheel Motor Applications: Hybrid Analytical Modeling (Reluctance Network and formal Analytical solution) and Inductance Measurements
authors: Imane Belmahi, Salim Asfirane, Georges Barakat, Ferhat Chabour, Yacine Amara
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: The aim of this paper is to study the possibilities of an axial-field switched reluctance machine. The analytical model established from the solution of Maxwell’s equations is developed and combined with a lumped parameter electromagnetic equivalent circuit model (expert reluctance network) to account for the analytical model’s lack of consideration of magnetic saturation of ferromagnetic material. This is based on the correction of air-gap flux density computed via the analytical model with the reluctance network, which significantly improves the results. A prototype has been built, and the tests allow for the evaluation of the capabilities of switched reluctance axial-field structures for in-wheel motor applications. Results on self-inductance estimation show that the Hybrid analytical model allows for corrections of the Analytical model showing a better agreement with measurements and three-dimensional finite element method model.

title: Distributional Stability of Tangent-Linearized Gaussian Inference on Smooth Manifolds
authors: Junghoon Seo, Hakjin Lee, Jaehoon Sim
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Gaussian inference on smooth manifolds is central to robotics, but exact marginalization and conditioning are generally non-Gaussian and geometry-dependent. We study tangent-linearized Gaussian inference and derive explicit non-asymptotic <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$W\_{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> stability bounds for projection marginalization and surface-measure conditioning. The bounds separate local second-order geometric distortion from nonlocal tail leakage and, for Gaussian inputs, yield closed-form diagnostics from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(\mu,\Sigma)$</tex-math></inline-formula> and curvature/reach surrogates. Circle and planar-pushing experiments validate the predicted calibration transition near <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\sqrt{\Vert \Sigma \Vert \_{\text{op}}}/R\approx 1/6$</tex-math></inline-formula> and indicate that normal-direction uncertainty is the dominant failure mode when locality breaks. These diagnostics provide practical triggers for switching from single-chart linearization to multi-chart or sample-based manifold inference. Code and Jupyter notebooks are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/mikigom/StabilityTLGaussian</uri>.

title: Capturability as Controlled-Invariant Sets: Recursive Feasibility for Variable-Stepping Time S2S NMPC
authors: Chao Song, Xizhe Zang, Boyang Chen, Yan Liu, Le Qi, Jie Zhao
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Capturability characterizes a safe region of states for humanoid walking and is most commonly constructed by analyzing the one-dimensional divergent component of motion (DCM) of the center of mass. In this work, by exploiting the mathematical structure of the step-to-step (S2S) dynamics, we characterize capturability directly at the S2S level using the notion of a controlled positive invariant set (CPIS), which is computed via backward reachable sets (BRS). Although the inclusion of step time as a control input makes the S2S dynamics bilinear and renders these BRS difficult to compute directly, we show that, thanks to the specific mathematical structure of the S2S model, the capturable CPIS of the original nonlinear system can be exactly represented by the controlled invariant set of a linear system obtained at the minimum step time $T\_{\min }$. Building on this set-valued characterization, we first propose a variable-step-time ALIP-based NMPC that employs the CPIS as a terminal set to guarantee recursive feasibility. We validated our approach through numerical simulations of the ALIP model, showing that an NMPC formulation equipped with a CPIS terminal set increases the feasibility (success) rate for challenging initial conditions near the boundary of the capturable set. We further demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed method via both simulation and real-world hardware experiments on the Bruce humanoid robot, as well as simulation experiments on the Unitree G1.

title:
A Phase Change Coupled Model for Plasma Arc Erosion on SF
                    <sub>6</sub>
                    Circuit Breaker Electrodes under Turbulent Flow
authors: Xinyuan Hong, Youyuan Wang, Yuxiang Leng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Electrode erosion caused by plasma arcs is a major factor limiting the electrical lifetime of high-voltage switching equipment. Accurate modeling of this process is therefore essential. However, experimental observations reveal diffuse and nearly uniform erosion on electrodes after current interruption, in contrast to the localized crater morphology simulated by established arc models. This study indicates the physical origin of this discrepancy is turbulent flow during the arcing process. To examine this hypothesis, a multi-physics simulation framework is developed, which coupled magnetohydrodynamics, a k–ε turbulence model, and phase-change of the electrode material. Comparisons under laminar and turbulent conditions demonstrate that strong turbulence acts as an important macroscopic factor affecting the erosion morphology and material evaporation. Experimental validation is conducted on a 252 kV self-blast SF<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sub> circuit breaker. The proposed model estimates the evaporated mass and length loss with errors below 3.43% and 3.56%, respectively. These results provide a reliable numerical tool and theoretical basis for electrical lifetime assessment and structural optimization of high-voltage switching equipment.

title: Over-the-Air Diagnosis of Defective Elements in Intelligent Reflecting Surface
authors: Ziyi Zhao, Zhaorui Wang, Lin Zhou, Chunsong Sun, Shuowen Zhang, Naofal Al-Dhahir, Liang Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Due to circuit failures, defective elements that cannot adaptively adjust the phase shifts of their impinging signals in a desired manner may exist on an intelligent reflecting surface (IRS). Traditional way to locate these defective IRS elements requires a thorough diagnosis of all the circuits belonging to a huge number of IRS elements, which is practically challenging. In this paper, we will devise novel approaches under which a transmitter sends known pilot signals and a receiver localizes all the defective IRS elements just based on its over-the-air measurements reflected from the IRS. Specifically, given any set of IRS elements, we propose an efficient method to process the received signals to determine whether this cluster contains defective elements or not with a very high accuracy probability. Based on this method, we show that the over-the-air diagnosis problem belongs to the 20 questions problem, where we can adaptively change the query set at the IRS so as to localize all the defective elements as quickly as possible. Along this line, we first propose a sorted posterior matching (sortPM) based method according to the noisy 20 questions technique, which enables accurate diagnosis even if the answers about the existence of defective elements in some sets of interest are wrong at certain question and answer (Q&A) rounds due to the noisy received signals. Next, to reduce the complexity, we propose a bisection based method according to the noiseless 20 questions technique, which totally trusts the answer at each Q&A round and keeps removing half of the remaining region based on such answers. Via numerical results, we show that our proposed methods can exploit the over-the-air measurements to localize all the defective IRS elements quickly and accurately.

title: Robust Manipulation of Deformable Linear Objects
authors: Jimmy Envall, Stelian Coros
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Robotic manipulation is a fundamental challenge in the pursuit of automating household tasks and advancing robotic manufacturing. Manipulation planning for deformable objects is particularly challenging due to the associated large action spaces and complex dynamics. For deformable objects, some actions are sensitive to noise in the model, which can significantly degrade the accuracy of predicted trajectories. In this letter, we present a motion planning algorithm for arranging deformable linear objects (DLOs) into user-provided goal configurations. Using a novel problem formulation as well as a combination of sampling and gradient based optimization, the algorithm finds sequences of grasps and control inputs that are robust. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the new algorithm using numerical experiments and manipulation examples both in simulation and in real world environments.

title: Tangram: Encryption-friendly SNARK framework under Pedersen committed engines_supp1-3691113.pdf
authors: Gweonho Jeong
publish: N/A
abstract: SNARKs are frequently used to prove encryption, yet the circuit size often becomes large due to the intricate operations inherent in encryption. It entails considerable computational overhead for a prover and can also lead to an increase in the size of the public parameters (e.g., evaluation key). We propose an encryption-friendly SNARK framework, Tangram, which allows anyone to construct a system by using their desired encryption and proof system. Our approach revises existing encryption schemes to produce Pedersen-like ciphertext, including identity-based, hierarchical identity-based, and attribute-based encryption. Afterward, to prove the knowledge of the encryption, we utilize a modular manner of commit-and-prove SNARKs, which uses commitment as a ‘bridge’. With our framework, one can prove encryption significantly faster than proving the whole encryption within the circuit. We implement various Tangram gadgets and evaluate their performance. Our results show 12x - 3500x times better performance than encryption-in-the-circuit.

title: Beyond Chunk-level RAG: Hierarchical Document-aware Retrieval for Indian Legal Judgments
authors: M Shunmuga Sundari, S Tejeswar, A Arivarasan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICDSAAI)
abstract: Legal texts are known to contain complex legal jargon, dense reasoning, and document structure, which may be hard to comprehend. Due to this, conventional Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) techniques could not produce optimal results on legal textS on various benchmarks. Legal queries often require the correct document for context, rather than relevant chunks across various documents, which plain RAG struggles with. In this paper, we propose a new hybrid document retrieval technique, which significantly improves the retrieval quality for legal judgments. The system consists of extracting legal judgments of the Supreme Court of India and generating concise, semantically rich summaries for each judgment to capture legal reasoning and important facts. Parallelly, each judgment is partitioned into coarse-grained chunks (larger-sized chunks) to preserve detailed factual and doctrinal nuances. Both the case summary and coarse chunks are ingested into separate vector databases. For a given query, the system retrieves candidate documents based on both summary-level similarity and chunk-level similarity. For a given document, the chunk-level score is the maximum similarity score of all its corresponding chunks retrieved. A final document relevancy score is computed as a weighted combination of the summary-level similarity score and the chunk-level score. The selected documents are partitioned into fine chunks (smaller chunks), which are passed to a reranking mechanism. Reranked fine chunks are used as context for answer generation.

title: Comparative Analysis using on Phishing URL’s using Machine Learning and Deep Learning
authors: Himanshu Heda, Mudita Sharan, Preety Shoran
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Recent Trends in Computing and Smart Mobility (RCSM)
abstract: Phishing is also a longstanding and active form of cybercrime, which latches onto unaware users and costs a lot in terms of money expense and reputation. Effective detection of phishing URLs can only be achieved through advanced innovative methods which can keep abreast with the tactics of the adversarial. In this paper, a comparative analysis of machine learning and deep learning phishing URL detection models is done in detail. The algorithm includes a set of stages wherein the scope of data collection, predate processing, feature engineering and hyperparameter tuning lead to a systematic testing of a wide range of models, including classical classifiers (Random Forest, SVM, XGBoost), stage 2 deep learning models LSTM, GRU + Biography and stage 3 hybrid systems Voting, Stacking, complex CNN->LSTM->GRU->BiGRU->RNN. The results of the experiment show that the hybrid Stacking Classifier performed best, with an accuracy of 97.70% and an F1-score of 97.65 that were highly superior as compared to individual models. The paper illustrates the Flexibility of ensemble and hybrid deep learning systems and indicates the efficiency of the system to establish a great degree of precision and defense against the existing cyber-crimes and infrastructural assaults.

title: AI-Enhanced Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems in the Age of Quantum Cyber Threats
authors: Tarun Kumar Vashishth, Manoj Kumar, Puneet Chauhan, Sharad Kumar, Jagdeep Singh, Vikas Sharma
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Recent Trends in Computing and Smart Mobility (RCSM)
abstract: The coming together of Artificial Intelligence (AI) with new Quantum Computing Technologies impacts security on all systems and networks. The new technologies can generate highly advanced attacks which cannot be prevented by any ordinary or non AI's based Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS). In this paper, the work presents AI enhanced IDPS systems based on machine learning models and deep learning for providing real time attack detection for each bit of expected normal data streams, where normal is determined by means of other learning based IDPS systems. In this consideration the major problem of Quantum Computing is seen, therefore new principles must be applied and introduce, and is suggested to be introduced to protect systems and networks to be able to fight against the Quantum attacks. The discussion that is presented in this work is to highlight the large need for pro-active, adaptable solutions to security for systems and networks which can provide systems protection against all present and future sophisticated means of attack, which cause we would means create new structures from which can make our systems more robust for the future systems which is required for maintenance of a digital infrastructure from which can provide a safe, secure and enjoyable human life.

title: Dynamic Modeling and Experimental Validation of ARMROV: Pitch/Roll Stability for Underwater Hull Cleaning
authors: Chiheb Zaoui, Saber Hachicha, Ahmed Mouhli, Tarek Azzabi, Maher Bouazizi, Habib Dallagi
publish: 2026 IEEE International conference on Advanced Systems and Emergent Technologies (IC_ASET)
abstract: Underwater hull cleaning prevents biofouling without dry-docking. This paper presents a Newton-Euler-based dynamic model that explicitly incorporates arm-vehicle coupling and nonlinear hydrodynamic effects, validated through systematic experimentation on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 / 5$</tex>-scale symmetric prototype with dual manipulator arms. The main contribution lies in the experimental quantification of pitch/roll disturbances from arm recoil and currents, and the validation of a PID-based compensation strategy achieving stabilization of the central body within 10 seconds under unidirectional and bidirectional perturbations. Simulations and basin experiments (pendulum system, IMU-6050) confirm effectiveness for cleaning operations.

title: A Multimodal Structural Intelligence Model for Formal Narrative Mapping Across Text and Film Domains
authors: R. Rajakumari, V. J. Bharathi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICDSAAI)
abstract: Understanding and structurally mapping narratives across text and film domains remains a significant challenge in multimodal learning due to variations in modality, sequencing, and symbolic interpretation. In this paper, the researcher presents MSI-Net (Multimodal Structural Intelligence Network), a new framework that aligns, integrates, and decentralises the decoding of narratives in literary texts and their film adaptations. The model is based on rare methods like Chronotope Tagging, Narrative Function Vectors, Scene Syntax Tree Embedding, and a Symbol-Aware Attention Module (SAAM), and it is capable of a) capturing linear plot progression and b) capturing symbolic motifs. Cross-modal affective alignment is provided by an Emotion-Resonance Synchronisation Module (ERSM) dedicated to this purpose. MSI-Net, evaluated on 30 paired narrative datasets, attained a narrative alignment accuracy of 90.1, a symbolic motif F1-score of 83.9, and a graph coherence index of 0.84, which is much better than the baselines. Its strength and structural generality were also proven when the model exhibited 85.7% accuracy in zero-shot generalisation to unseen story pairs. MSI-Net also offers an effective means of comparative storytelling, adaptive media generation and narrative AI applications by decoding stories into cross-modal narrative graphs. The study fills an essential gap in multimodal knowledge and paves the way for future systems capable of understanding stories across movement, time, medium, and culture.

title: Multi-Service Routing Optimization in SDN Based on Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Lipeng Xing, Chengjin Wang, Youwei Shi, Kouquan Zheng
publish: 2026 IEEE 8th International Conference on Communications, Information System and Computer Engineering (CISCE)
abstract: Emerging applications impose increasingly stringent Quality of Service (QoS) requirements on modern networks. Benefiting from the control-data decoupling and inherent programmability of Software-Defined Networking (SDN), fine-grained traffic engineering can be performed to support heterogeneous application demands. However, existing routing schemes still struggle to achieve efficient path optimization under diverse service constraints. To mitigate this issue, we propose a routing algorithm based on Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO-R). The model employs fully connected layers to extract implicit features from network topologies and traffic patterns, strengthening representation ability and generalization over complex network states. Moreover, differentiated reward functions are designed to match various service types, allowing iterative policy optimization via continuous interaction with the network environment. This design enables QoS-aware routing decisions while maintaining strong adaptability to dynamic traffic variations. We implement MAPPO-R in an SDN framework and evaluate its performance on the Mininet platform with realistic topologies and traffic traces. Experimental results show that the proposed algorithm effectively satisfies heterogeneous QoS constraints across multiple service classes, with superior adaptability and generalization in high-load network scenarios.

title: Integrating Psychoanalysis with AI for Cognitive Threat Detection in Cyber Environments
authors: Kumari Priyanka Sinha, L. Bhagyalakshmi, T.Haripriya, Irfan Bagawan, Swathi Voddi, Sanjay Kumar Suman
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Recent Trends in Computing and Smart Mobility (RCSM)
abstract: Neuro-Symbolic Adaptive Resonance Framework (NSARF) presented in this research briefs provides the distinctiveness of a project proposed to obtain the cyber identifying threats by merging the advanced modelling (cognition) and artificial intelligence. Conventional cyber defensive strategies are largely geared towards recognizing anomalies that could be identified and signature-like attack mechanism is not always capable of detecting some covert KB of malicious intent. NSARF can address this problem by simulating fundamental cognitive/emotional mechanisms that can assist this solution in determining aggressive real intentionality and the behavior of predicting future assault techniques. The parts of the offered framework are a behaviour-based encoder (the hidden physiological signals), the element of neural representation fusion (combining deep learning extractor and symbol evaluation), and the flexible psychological resonant three-part (perpetual adjustment of the identification and mitigation actions in real-time). This is carried out in layers which not only makes it difficult to be detected, but is also available to a system which is not rivaled in viewing information on sub-reactance goals and tactics that cyber criminals be transporting in their attack. Analytical assessments reveal that NSARF is not less comparable to the classical methods in the sense of detecting and recognising complex and not obvious threats by low levels of false positive rates. The suggested way is an effective solution to meet the contemporary cybersecurity issues - flexible, robust and transparent - as it integrates cognitive analysis with human-like approach and ML. NSARF is a notable forward step in the automated technologies, which can identify threats pro-actively, that can suit rapidly changing emerging and even in highly risky cyber settings. The techniques of NSARF had reached the level of 94.5 percent total accuracy.

title: Protecting Healthcare Networks from Cyber Threats: How to Use AI and Blockchain to Enable Safe Patient Data Exchange
authors: Shamshad Ahmed Khan, Faiz Ahmed Khan, R. Ravichandran
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICDSAAI)
abstract: Healthcare digitisation has accelerated in response to rising cybersecurity threats, large-scale data breaches, and heightened privacy concerns during and after the COVID-19 era. Despite the adoption of centralised authentication, encryption, and other conventional safeguards, healthcare systems continue to face persistent risks, including ransomware, insider threats, and phishing attacks. This has motivated the shift toward decentralised, intelligent security architectures built on artificial intelligence and blockchain technologies. AI-enabled threat-detection systems use machine-learning algorithms to identify and mitigate cyber risks in real time. Comparative evaluations of Support Vector Machines (SVM), Random Forest, XGBoost, and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks show that deep-learning approaches-particularly LSTM-significantly outperform traditional methods in detecting complex malware patterns. Blockchain-based security architectures further enhance protection by ensuring data integrity, enabling secure access control, and establishing decentralised trust across private, public, and hybrid blockchain environments. Federated learning adds another layer of privacy preservation by allowing AI models to be trained on distributed healthcare datasets without requiring direct access to sensitive patient information. Experimental results demonstrate that hybrid AI-blockchain frameworks achieve over $98 \%$ accuracy in identifying cyber threats while substantially reducing attack vectors and unauthorised-access incidents. Several challenges remain before widespread adoption is feasible. Blockchain scalability, AI computation overhead, and regulatory compliance issues must be addressed-particularly in sectors such as the healthcare supply chain, where operational constraints are strict. Future work may focus on optimising AI algorithms for real-time monitoring, integrating quantum-resistant cryptography to prepare for emerging threats, and improving interoperability across heterogeneous blockchain platforms.

title: Analysis of the Impact of Core BP/LTP Protocol Parameters on Transmission Performance in Satellite Communications
authors: Yingxiang Han, Mingyue Bian, Zichen Yu, Suzhi Cao, Lei Yan
publish: 2026 IEEE 8th International Conference on Communications, Information System and Computer Engineering (CISCE)
abstract: Addressing the characteristics of long delay, high bit error rate, and dynamic interruption in space communications, the BP/LTP protocol stack serves as a critical transmission solution within the DTN-ION architecture, where its core parameter configuration directly affects transmission performance. This paper first systematically disassembles the layered encapsulation and cooperative transmission mechanisms of the BP/LTP protocol, clarifying the generation logic and interaction constraints of the Bundle, Block, and Segment. Secondly, we develop a mathematical model of business delivery time based on geometric distribution and extreme-value statistics. The model quantitatively captures the coupled effects of bundle size, block size, and segment size together with link characteristics, including bit error rate, propagation delay, and transmission rate, on the resulting performance metrics. Finally, a dual-node direct connection experimental platform is built based on the Phytium D2000 processor. Through single-factor variable experiments, the validity of the model is verified, and the influence laws of core parameters on business delivery time and effective throughput are explored. The experimental results demonstrate that the performance trends of the theoretical model and the physical experiments agree well, thus clarifying the optimal configuration intervals and constraint boundaries for each parameter. In the scenario of satellite symmetric link transmission of large-scale business data communication, the optimal interval for Bundle size is 10 kB or more, with the maximum size constrained by the underlying Block encapsulation; Block size performs better with large-sized data packets in big data scenarios, but the delivery latency of small data packets must be considered, preventing infinite increase in Block size; Segment size is subject to the triple core constraints of protocol mechanisms, link characteristics, and engineering implementation, requiring a balance between performance optimization and feasibility. This study provides precise theoretical support and experimental basis for parameter configuration of the BP/LTP protocol in deep space communication scenarios.

title: Micro-Expressions with Swin-T on CASME II: Spatial Heads and Temporal Modelling
authors: Navaneeth Krishnan, Onkar Mane, Anushka Raspayle, Sakshi Indolia
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Recent Trends in Computing and Smart Mobility (RCSM)
abstract: Microexpressions are fast and tiny facial expressions that last for only a split second but reveal true emotions shown on the face. Identifying microexpressions (MER) is really important in several fields, including lie detection, security screening, psychotherapy etc. Identifying microexpressions still remains tough due to their small duration, subtle intensity, and the small size of available datasets. In this paper, we train Swin Transformer Tiny (Swin-T) on CASME II (a MER dataset) utilizing its hierarchical self-attention in comparison to traditional CNNs. Additionaly, we implement it via two different methods: Spatial and Temporal. For the spatial implementation, we replace the default classifier of Swin-T with lightweight Conv-Norm-GELU blocks, allowing the model to increase the robust local facial features delicately. For the temporal implementation, we add a small GRU/1D-Conv head to the model backbone that aggregates motion-derived information across a brief sequence of onset–apex–offset (O/A/O) frames, allowing the model to observe dynamic changes in the face across time. Furthermore, we compare both the implementations with ImageNet-1k and RAF-DB (Macro-expression dataset) pretraining in order to examine the contribution of general versus domain-specific prior knowledge. Experiments on our benchmark CASME II dataset demonstrate that temporal implementation coupled with RAF-DB pretraining results in 91.28% accuracy and macro-F1 of 0.9087, both beating our spatial implementation and previous state-of-the-art techniques in MER. This paper gives a simple, reproducible Swin-T configuration and a comparative study demonstrating when temporal cues and domain-specific pretraining make the greatest contribution toward MER.
